Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel The Deposition Of James D. Waggoner; Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $1,300.00 Against Petitioner Progressive Insurance, Adam K. Tanouye, Esq., Peter J. Hirsig, Esq. From The Law Offices Of Mcnamara, Ambacher,Wheeler, Hirsig & Gray Llp; Declaration Of Alberto Daniel Ramos
Off Calendar Mar-26-2026 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or Alternatively Motion For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Mar-11-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PETITIONER'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR ALTERNATIVELY MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Appearances via Zoom:
Adam Tanouye of McNamara Ambacher Wheeler Hirsig & Gray LLP (707.427.3998, Adam.Tanouye@mcnamaralaw.com) for petitioner and moving party;
Alberto Ramos of Windsor Troy Law LLP (323.800.5405, litigation@wtroy.com) for respondent and opposing party.

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Petitioner Progressive Insurance's motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied.

Petitioner failed to maintain its burden of production pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure, Sec. 437c, subdivision (p)(2). Petitioner contends that respondent's underinsured motorist claim is excluded from the ambit of its policy because the accident occurred during the course and scope of respondent's employment. The motion fails since petitioner does not present competent evidence to support the assertion. The court sustains respondent's objections to the Tanouye Decl., Exs. C [recorded interview] and F [chiropractic form]. These documents purportedly show that respondent admitted that he was working when the accident occurred. But Tanouye is petitioner's counsel and he fails to properly authenticate the exhibits. His declaration provides no personal knowledge that the documents are what they purport to be.

The court also sustains respondent's objection to the reply evidence (Waggoner Decl.). The general rule is that new reply evidence is unpermitted. (Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1522, 1537 ["The general rule of motion practice... is that new evidence is not permitted with reply papers. This principle is most prominent in the context of summary judgment motions..."]; San Diego Watercrafts, Inc v. Wells Fargo Bank (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308, 316 [noting evidence should be cited in the separate statement of undisputed facts].) The Legislature has recently clarified that the court may not rely on reply evidence to grant summary judgment. (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 437c, subd. (b)(4) ["The reply shall not include any new evidentiary matter, additional material facts, or separate statement submitted with the reply and not presented in the moving papers or opposing papers."].)

Lastly, even if the court accepted petitioner's evidence, there is a triable issue of material fact whether respondent was working when the accident occurred. At deposition, respondent unequivocally testified that he did not work on the morning of the accident. (Respondent's Evidence, Ex. A [Renteria Depo., 31:23-32:21].) Petitioner cites D'Amico v. Board of Medical Examiners (1974) 11 Cal.3d 1 and asserts that the court can disregard this subsequent testimony. The court disagrees. D'Amico explains that a party cannot avoid summary judgment by providing a declaration that contradicts prior deposition admissions. (Id., at pp. 21-22.) The conflicting recorded interview, and the chiropracter's intake form, are not sworn. They are not on equal footing with sworn deposition testimony. The court therefore does not disregard the deposition testimony; rather, the testimony creates a triable issue of fact that precludes summary judgment.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Christine Van Aken, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Jane Hong, CSR 11975 (T: 909.831.2644, E: CSRJane@hotmail.com)
